During the war, the armies of Western Allied nations such as Australia, Canada, the UK and the US[[109]](#cite_note-112) were given orders to treat [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers_of_World_War_II "Axis powers of World War II") prisoners strictly in accordance with the [Geneva Convention](/wiki/Geneva_Convention_on_Prisoners_of_War_(1929) "Geneva Convention on Prisoners of War (1929)").[[110]](#cite_note-113) Some breaches of the Convention took place, however. According to [Stephen E. Ambrose](/wiki/Stephen_E._Ambrose "Stephen E. Ambrose"), of the roughly 1,000 US combat veterans he had interviewed, only one admitted to shooting a prisoner, saying he "felt remorse, but would do it again". However, one-third of interviewees told him they had seen fellow US troops kill German prisoners.[[111]](#cite_note-114)

In Britain, German prisoners, particularly higher-ranked officers, were housed in luxurious buildings where [listening devices](/wiki/Covert_listening_device "Covert listening device") were installed. A considerable amount of military intelligence was gained from [eavesdropping](/wiki/Eavesdropping "Eavesdropping") on what the officers believed were private casual conversations. Much of the listening was carried out by German refugees, in many cases Jews. The work of these refugees in contributing to the Allied victory was declassified over half a century later.[[112]](#cite_note-115)

In February 1944, 59.7% of POWs in America were employed. This relatively low percentage was due to problems setting wages that would not compete against those of non-prisoners, to union opposition, as well as concerns about security, sabotage, and escape. Given national manpower shortages, citizens and employers resented the idle prisoners, and efforts were made to decentralise the camps and reduce security enough that more prisoners could work. By the end of May 1944, POW employment was at 72.8%, and by late April 1945 it had risen to 91.3%. The sector that made the most use of POW workers was agriculture. There was more demand than supply of prisoners throughout the war, and 14,000 POW repatriations were delayed in 1946 so prisoners could be used in the spring farming seasons, mostly to thin and block [sugar beets](/wiki/Sugar_beets "Sugar beets") in the west. While some in Congress wanted to extend POW labour beyond June 1946, President Truman rejected this, leading to the end of the program.[[113]](#cite_note-histpow-116)

Towards the end of the war in Europe, as large numbers of Axis soldiers surrendered, the US created the designation of [Disarmed Enemy Forces](/wiki/Disarmed_Enemy_Forces "Disarmed Enemy Forces") (DEF) so as not to treat prisoners as POWs. A lot of these soldiers were kept in open fields in makeshift camps in the Rhine valley (*[Rheinwiesenlager](/wiki/Rheinwiesenlager "Rheinwiesenlager")*). Controversy has arisen about how Eisenhower managed these prisoners.[[114]](#cite_note-117) (see *[Other Losses](/wiki/Other_Losses "Other Losses")*).

After the surrender of Germany in May 1945, the POW status of the German prisoners was in many cases maintained, and they were for several years used as public labourers in countries such as the UK and France. Many died when forced to clear minefields in countries such as Norway and France. "By September 1945 it was estimated by the French authorities that two thousand prisoners were being maimed and killed each month in accidents".[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119)

In 1946, the UK held over 400,000 German POWs, many having been transferred from POW camps in the US and Canada. They were employed as labourers to compensate for the lack of manpower in Britain, as a form of [war reparation](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations").[[117]](#cite_note-Renate_Held_2008-120)[[118]](#cite_note-121) A public debate ensued in the UK over the treatment of German prisoners of war, with many in Britain comparing the treatment to the POWs to [slave labour](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery").[[119]](#cite_note-German_migrants-122) In 1947, the Ministry of Agriculture argued against repatriation of working German prisoners, since by then they made up 25 per cent of the land workforce, and it wanted to continue having them work in the UK until 1948.[[119]](#cite_note-German_migrants-122)

The "[London Cage](/wiki/London_Cage "London Cage")", an [MI19](/wiki/MI19 "MI19") prisoner of war facility in London used during and immediately after the war to interrogate prisoners before sending them to prison camps, was subject to allegations of torture.[[120]](#cite_note-Secrets-123)

After the German surrender, the International Red Cross was prohibited from providing aid, such as food or prisoner visits, to POW camps in Germany. However, after making appeals to the Allies in the autumn of 1945, the Red Cross was allowed to investigate the camps in the British and French occupation zones of Germany, as well as providing relief to the prisoners held there.[[121]](#cite_note-autogenerated2005-124) On 4 February 1946, the Red Cross was also permitted to visit and assist prisoners in the US occupation zone of Germany, although only with very small quantities of food. "During their visits, the delegates observed that German prisoners of war were often detained in appalling conditions. They drew the attention of the authorities to this fact, and gradually succeeded in getting some improvements made".[[121]](#cite_note-autogenerated2005-124)

POWs were also transferred among the Allies, with for example 6,000 German officers transferred from Western Allied camps to the Soviets and subsequently imprisoned in the [Sachsenhausen concentration camp](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_concentration_camp "Sachsenhausen concentration camp"), at the time one of the [NKVD special camps](/wiki/NKVD_special_camps_in_Germany_1945%E2%80%9350 "NKVD special camps in Germany 1945–50").[[122]](#cite_note-125)[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-127) Although the Soviet Union had not signed the Geneva Convention, the U.S. chose to hand over several hundred thousand German prisoners to the Soviet Union in May 1945 as a "gesture of friendship".[[125]](#cite_note-128) U.S. forces also refused to accept the surrender of German troops attempting to surrender to them in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") and [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), and handed them over to the Soviet Union instead.[[126]](#cite_note-129)

The United States handed over 740,000 German prisoners to France, which was a Geneva Convention signatory but which used them as forced labourers. Newspapers reported that the POWs were being mistreated; Judge [Robert H. Jackson](/wiki/Robert_H._Jackson "Robert H. Jackson"), chief US prosecutor in the [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials"), told US President [Harry S Truman](/wiki/Harry_S_Truman "Harry S Truman") in October 1945 that the Allies themselves,

> have done or are doing some of the very things we are prosecuting the Germans for. The French are so violating the Geneva Convention in the treatment of prisoners of war that our command is taking back prisoners sent to them. We are prosecuting plunder and our Allies are practising it.[[127]](#cite_note-130)[[128]](#cite_note-131)

#### Hungarians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prisoner_of_war&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Hungarians")]

[Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") became POWs of the Western Allies. Some of these were, like the Germans, used as forced labour in France after the cessation of hostilities.[[129]](#cite_note-132) After the war, Hungarian POWs were handed over to the Soviets and transported to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") for [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labor_of_Hungarians_in_the_Soviet_Union "Forced labor of Hungarians in the Soviet Union"). Such forced Hungarian labour by the USSR is often referred to as [malenkij robot](/wiki/Malenkij_robot "Malenkij robot")—little work. [András Toma](/wiki/Andr%C3%A1s_Toma "András Toma"), a Hungarian soldier taken prisoner by the Red Army in 1944, was discovered in a Russian psychiatric hospital in 2000. It is likely that he was the last prisoner of war from World War II to be repatriated.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

#### Japanese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prisoner_of_war&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Japanese")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/OkinawaJapanesePOW.jpg/250px-OkinawaJapanesePOW.jpg)](/wiki/File:OkinawaJapanesePOW.jpg)

A group of Japanese soldiers captured during the [Battle of Okinawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Okinawa "Battle of Okinawa")

Although thousands of Japanese servicemembers were taken prisoner of war, most fought until they were killed or committed suicide. Of the 22,000 Japanese soldiers present at the beginning of the [Battle of Iwo Jima](/wiki/Battle_of_Iwo_Jima "Battle of Iwo Jima"), over 20,000 were killed and only 216 were taken prisoner of war.[[131]](#cite_note-134) Of the 30,000 Japanese troops that defended [Saipan](/wiki/Saipan "Saipan"), fewer than 1,000 remained alive at battle's end.[[132]](#cite_note-135) Japanese prisoners of war sent to camps fared well; however, some were killed when attempting to surrender or were massacred[[133]](#cite_note-136) just after doing so (see [Allied war crimes during World War II in the Pacific](/wiki/Allied_war_crimes_during_World_War_II#The_Pacific "Allied war crimes during World War II")). In some instances, Japanese prisoners of war were tortured through a variety of methods.[[134]](#cite_note-cnn19969623-137) A method of torture used by the Chinese [National Revolutionary Army](/wiki/National_Revolutionary_Army "National Revolutionary Army") (NRA) included suspending prisoners by the neck in wooden cages until they died.[[135]](#cite_note-138) In very rare cases, some were beheaded by sword, and a severed head was once used as a football by Chinese National Revolutionary Army (NRA) soldiers.[[136]](#cite_note-139)

After the war, many Japanese POWs were kept on as [Japanese Surrendered Personnel](/wiki/Japanese_Surrendered_Personnel "Japanese Surrendered Personnel") until mid-1947 by the Allies. The JSP were used until 1947 for labour purposes, such as road maintenance, recovering corpses for reburial, cleaning, and preparing farmland. Early tasks also included repairing airfields damaged by Allied bombing during the war and maintaining law and order until the arrival of Allied forces in the region.

#### Italians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prisoner_of_war&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Italians")]

In 1943, Italy overthrew [Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") and became an Allied co-belligerent. This did not change the status of many Italian POWs, retained in [Australia](/wiki/Italian_prisoners_of_war_in_Australia "Italian prisoners of war in Australia"), the UK and US due to labour shortages.[[137]](#cite_note-140)

After [Italy surrendered to the Allies](/wiki/Armistice_of_Cassibile "Armistice of Cassibile") and declared war on Germany, the United States initially made plans to send Italian POWs back to fight Germany. Ultimately though, the government decided instead to loosen POW work requirements prohibiting Italian prisoners from carrying out war-related work. About 34,000 Italian POWs were active in 1944 and 1945 on 66 US military installations, performing support roles such as quartermaster, repair, and engineering work as [Italian Service Units](/wiki/Italian_Service_Units "Italian Service Units").[[113]](#cite_note-histpow-116)

#### Cossacks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prisoner_of_war&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Cossacks")]

On 11 February 1945, at the conclusion of the [Yalta Conference](/wiki/Yalta_Conference "Yalta Conference"), the United States and United Kingdom signed a Repatriation Agreement with the USSR.[[138]](#cite_note-141) The interpretation of this agreement resulted in the forcible repatriation of all Soviets ([Operation Keelhaul](/wiki/Operation_Keelhaul "Operation Keelhaul")) regardless of their wishes. The forced repatriation operations took place in 1945–1947.[[139]](#cite_note-142)

## Post-World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prisoner_of_war&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Post-World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Americanexecuted1950korea.jpg/250px-Americanexecuted1950korea.jpg)](/wiki/File:Americanexecuted1950korea.jpg)

A U.S. Army POW of the [21st Infantry Regiment.](/wiki/21st_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "21st Infantry Regiment (United States)") bound and killed by North Koreans during the Korean War.

During the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), the North Koreans developed a reputation for torturing and severely mistreating prisoners of war (see [Treatment of POWs by North Korean and Chinese forces](/wiki/Korean_War_POWs_detained_in_North_Korea#Treatment_of_POWs_by_North_Korean_and_Chinese_forces "Korean War POWs detained in North Korea")). Their POWs were housed in three camps, according to their potential usefulness to the North Korean army. Peace camps and reform camps were for POWs that were either sympathetic to the cause or who had valued skills that could be useful to the North Korean military; these enemy soldiers were indoctrinated and sometimes conscripted into the North Korean army. While POWs in peace camps were reportedly treated with more consideration,[[140]](#cite_note-Three-143) regular prisoners of war were usually tortured or treated very poorly.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/USVietPeace.JPG/250px-USVietPeace.JPG)](/wiki/File:USVietPeace.JPG)

An American POW being released by North Vietnamese and Viet Cong captors in February 1973.

The 1952 Inter-Camp POW Olympics were held from 15 to 27 November 1952 in Pyuktong, [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"). The Chinese hoped to gain worldwide publicity, and while some prisoners refused to participate, some 500 POWs of eleven nationalities took part.[[141]](#cite_note-Adams,_2007,_p._62-144) They came from all the North Korean prison camps and competed in football, baseball, softball, basketball, volleyball, track and field, soccer, gymnastics, and [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing").[[141]](#cite_note-Adams,_2007,_p._62-144) For the POWs, this was also an opportunity to meet with friends from other camps. The prisoners had their own photographers, announcers, and even reporters, who after each day's competition published a newspaper, the "Olympic Roundup".[[142]](#cite_note-145)

At the end of the [First Indochina War](/wiki/First_Indochina_War "First Indochina War"), of the 11,721 French soldiers taken prisoner after the [Battle of Dien Bien Phu](/wiki/Battle_of_Dien_Bien_Phu "Battle of Dien Bien Phu") and led by the [Viet Minh](/wiki/Viet_Minh "Viet Minh") on [death marches](/wiki/Death_march "Death march") to distant POW camps, only 3,290 were repatriated four months later.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Hanoi-taxi-march1973.jpg/250px-Hanoi-taxi-march1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hanoi-taxi-march1973.jpg)

Recently released American POWs from North Vietnamese prison camps in 1973.

During the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), the [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") and [North Vietnamese Army](/wiki/North_Vietnamese_Army "North Vietnamese Army") took many [South Vietnamese](/wiki/South_Vietnamese_Army "South Vietnamese Army") and [United States servicemembers as prisoners of war](/wiki/U.S._prisoners_of_war_during_the_Vietnam_War "U.S. prisoners of war during the Vietnam War") and subjected them to mistreatment and torture. Some American and South Vietnamese prisoners of war were held in the prison known to US POWs as the [Hanoi Hilton](/wiki/Hanoi_Hilton "Hanoi Hilton"). North Vietnamese and Viet Cong held in custody by South Vietnamese and American forces [were also tortured](/wiki/C%C3%B4n_%C4%90%E1%BA%A3o_Prison#Vietnam_War "Côn Đảo Prison") and badly treated.[[144]](#cite_note-south-viet-147) After the war, millions of South Vietnamese servicemen and government workers were sent to ["re-education" camps](/wiki/Re-education_camp_(Vietnam) "Re-education camp (Vietnam)"), where many perished.

As in previous conflicts, speculation existed, without evidence, that a handful of American pilots captured during the Korean and Vietnam wars were transferred to the Soviet Union and never repatriated.[[145]](#cite_note-148)[[146]](#cite_note-149)[[147]](#cite_note-150)

Regardless of regulations determining treatment of prisoners, violations of their rights continue to be reported. Many cases of POW massacres have been reported in recent times, including the murder of Israeli prisoners of war in the 1973 [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War") by their Egyptian captors, the [13 October massacre](/w/index.php?title=13_October_massacre&action=edit&redlink=1 "13 October massacre (page does not exist)") in Lebanon by Syrian forces and [June 1990 massacre](/wiki/Massacre_of_police_officers_in_Eastern_Sri_Lanka_in_June_1990 "Massacre of police officers in Eastern Sri Lanka in June 1990") in Sri Lanka.

Indian intervention in the [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War") in 1971 led to the [third Indo-Pakistan war](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971"), which ended in Indian victory and the capture of 93,000 Pakistani POWs, they were later slowly repatriated in a deal with Pakistani President [Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto").[[148]](#cite_note-151)

In 1982, during the [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War"), prisoners were well-treated in general by both sides, with military commanders dispatching enemy prisoners back to their homelands in record time following the end of the war.[[149]](#cite_note-152)

In 1991, during the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), American, British, Italian, and Kuwaiti POWs (mostly crew members of downed aircraft and special forces) were tortured by the Iraqi secret police. An American military doctor, [Major Rhonda Cornum](/wiki/Rhonda_Cornum "Rhonda Cornum"), a 37-year-old flight surgeon captured when her Blackhawk UH-60 was shot down, was also subjected to sexual abuse.[[150]](#cite_note-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Yugoslav_EPWs_detained_in_Kosovo_by_the_USMC%27s_26th_MEU_%28July_1999%29.jpg/250px-Yugoslav_EPWs_detained_in_Kosovo_by_the_USMC%27s_26th_MEU_%28July_1999%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yugoslav_EPWs_detained_in_Kosovo_by_the_USMC%27s_26th_MEU_(July_1999).jpg)

[Yugoslav](/wiki/Ground_Forces_of_Serbia_and_Montenegro "Ground Forces of Serbia and Montenegro") POWs during the [Kosovo War](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War") in 1999.

During the [Yugoslav Wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars") in the 1990s, [Serb](/wiki/Serb "Serb") paramilitary forces supported by [JNA](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army") forces killed POWs at [Vukovar](/wiki/Vukovar_massacre "Vukovar massacre") and [Škarbrnja](/wiki/%C5%A0kabrnja_massacre "Škabrnja massacre"), while [Bosnian Serb](/wiki/Bosnian_Serb "Bosnian Serb") forces killed POWs at [Srebrenica](/wiki/Srebrenica_massacre "Srebrenica massacre"). A large number of surviving Croatian or Bosnian POWs described the conditions in Serbian concentration camps as similar to those in Germany in World War II, including regular beatings, torture and random executions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2001, reports emerged concerning two POWs that India had taken during the [Sino-Indian War](/wiki/Sino-Indian_War "Sino-Indian War"), Yang Chen and Shih Liang. The two were imprisoned as spies for three years before being interned in a [mental asylum](/wiki/Mental_asylum "Mental asylum") in [Ranchi](/wiki/Ranchi "Ranchi"), where they spent the following 38 years under a special prisoner status.[[151]](#cite_note-154)

The last prisoners of the 1980–1988 [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War") were exchanged in 2003.[[152]](#cite_note-155)

During the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)"), Ukrainian POWs have described being tortured by [Russian forces](/wiki/Russian_Armed_Forces "Russian Armed Forces") using electrocution, beatings, and sexual abuse. Both sides of the conflict forced prisoners to be naked at times as a humiliating punishment.[[153]](#cite_note-156) According to the Israeli human rights group [B'tselem](/wiki/B%27tselem "B'tselem"), since the outbreak of the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") in October 2023, the abuse of [Palestinian detainees](/wiki/Mass_detentions_in_the_Gaza_war "Mass detentions in the Gaza war") has become so institutionalized that the prisons should be called 'torture camps'.[[154]](#cite_note-157)

## Numbers of POWs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prisoner_of_war&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Numbers of POWs")]

This section lists nations with the highest number of POWs since the start of World War II and ranked by descending order. These are also the highest numbers in any war since the [Convention Relative to the Treatment of Prisoners of War](/wiki/Geneva_Convention_on_Prisoners_of_War_(1929) "Geneva Convention on Prisoners of War (1929)") entered into force on 19 June 1931. The [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR") had not signed the Geneva Convention.[[155]](#cite_note-158)

| Army | Number of POWs held in captivity | War |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") | * About 3 million taken by USSR (474,967 died in captivity (>15%))[[156]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-159) Historian [Rüdiger Overmans](/wiki/R%C3%BCdiger_Overmans "Rüdiger Overmans") maintains that it seems entirely plausible, while not provable, that one million died in Soviet custody. He also believes that there were men who actually died as POWs among those listed as missing-in-action.[[157]](#cite_note-160) * Unknown number in [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") (the death rate for German prisoners of war was highest in Yugoslavia with over 50%)[[158]](#cite_note-161) * Over 4.5 million taken by the Western Allies before the formal surrender of Germany, another three million after the surrender[[d]](#cite_note-162) * 1.3 million unknown[[159]](#cite_note-163) | [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") |
| [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") | 5.7 million taken by Germany (about 3 [million died in captivity](/wiki/German_mistreatment_of_Soviet_prisoners_of_war "German mistreatment of Soviet prisoners of war") (56–68%))[[156]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-159) | World War II (total) |
| [France](/wiki/France "France") | 1,800,000 taken by Germany | World War II |
| [Republic of China](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") | 1,000,000+ taken by Japan[[160]](#cite_note-164)[[e]](#cite_note-165) | World War II |
| [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") | 675,000 (420,000 taken by Germany; 240,000 taken by the Soviets in 1939; 15,000 taken by Germany in Warsaw in 1944) | World War II |
| [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") | ≈200,000 (135,000 taken in Europe, does not include Pacific or Commonwealth figures) | World War II |
| [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") | ≈175,000 taken by [Coalition of the Gulf War](/wiki/Coalition_of_the_Gulf_War "Coalition of the Gulf War") | [Persian Gulf War](/wiki/Persian_Gulf_War "Persian Gulf War") |
| [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") | * 114,861 lost or captured by US and UK * 60,000 captured by [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") | World War II |
| [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") | ≈130,000 (95,532 taken by Germany) | World War II |
| [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") | * 16,000–50,000 captured by Western Allies * 560,000–760,000 captured by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), of them, it is estimated that between 60,000 and 347,000 died in captivity[[104]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-107)[[105]](#cite_note-UN_Press_Release-108) | World War II |
